item.title: Re: Licensing Captain Post
item.content:encoded: Gotcha, thanks for clarifying.

item.title: Licensing Captain Post
item.content:encoded: That's all copyrighted. Don't do this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I recently got the box set of Season 5. If someone could tell me which episode is the one that starts it all off, I can create a fitting image of the correct disc, with writing, and release it under CC for the skip.

Or would I not be allowed to for some reason, like even if I take the photo, it would still include imagery that is copyrighted, in the art for the disc?

item.title: Re: Mispelling?
item.content:encoded: Odd, but makes sense. Thanks.

item.title: Re: Mispelling?
item.content:encoded: It's most likely meant to be "mimetic" (i.e. the nested narratives "mime" the general structure of narratives in the level of reality above theirs).

item.title: Mispelling?
item.content:encoded: I noticed that several times the word "memetic" is spelled as "mimetic". Is there something here I'm missing, or is this a spelling error?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Beautifully captures the imagination, giving a childlike sense of curiosity and discovery. Who wouldn't want to explore their favorite settings to such a degree? It's a real joy to read, and doesn't get so overtly creepy as to ruin the wonder — just enough to inspire more curiosity. Really really fantastic article :)

item.title: Re: Suggestions?
item.content:encoded: The page is not tagged "collaboration", it is not open for anyone to edit new logs in. If you have ideas for new material to add to the log, you will need permission from the original author to add that material.

item.title: Suggestions?
item.content:encoded: Is it okay if we add more test logs?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. The Sopranos JPEG always looked terrible on this article, and it never even matched what was being described.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Y'know, even without the recognizable but abnormally large low-quality jpeg of a Sopranos DVD Box, the whole article actually reads a lot better without it all being pushed to one side.

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC-BY-SA 3.0) as it orginates from this source, which states that it's owned by HBO, and is thus non-compliant. Thus, the image has been removed. The second image used is from here and is cc-compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article still unsettles me more than almost anything on the wiki. I can't enunciate why in any understandable way. It just does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is still one of my favorite SCPs on the site, and probably the one that scares me the most. It's beyond incredible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wtf man. Just when I thought this can't go anymore terrifying…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The demiurge is a "blind" pseudo-deity found in Gnosticism and Christian apocrypha/demonology, that falsely claims to be God. It's basically Descartes's "Evil_demon": an entity falsifying reality to deceive mortals.

In short, the Foundation thinks they maybe just saw the Matrix, and don't want to poke it in case they're right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get the first part of the caption on the hallway picture. The second part is obviously "Don't do that again or else," but what the hell does "DATA CONCURRENT WITH PATAPHYSICAL HYPERMODEL: DMRG" mean?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is it me or is the last one supposed to be a reference to swans proposal?
EDIT: nope. It is probably 398

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This really shouldn't be as creepy as it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this one. Any exploration-style SCP that manages to be this eerie purely on account of low-key yet persistent weirdness, never needing to fall back on any truly menacing presence or implication, is a winner in my book.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted at "POV was immediately directed to descend beneath the setting's thick atmospheric cloud cover," because that's absolutely what you do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The caption on that last picture is ineffably terrifying, as is the picture itself. An eternal hallway …

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hold on.

The image of the hallway is the exact same one used in SCP-398.

Is this a problem?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, I would love to see what this thing would show after the final scene of Lost.

(To reiterate, I would LOVE a collab log for this. Since the author is banned, could the mods consider authorizating one?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an excellent and refreshing take on the usual, repetitive exploration logs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wasn't very interesting, I found it somewhat boring and I feel it dragged on for too long. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this sentence is mistyped:

Researchers were direcIt was revealed the princess' stepmother is a dark sorceress and had been manipulating the events to ensure the crown for her youngest son.

Also, you used the term "Martins" instead of "Jacksons" in the Jetsons one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's the idea, actually. There's nothing that intrinsically triggers the spoops by diving into canons that only exist in the parent setting. I just wanted that novelty of "oh boy, we're entering whole universes that don't exist in our world, what's gonna happen?" instead of entering The Godfather through Goodfellas and OH DIP EVERYONE AT THE CORLEONE WEDDING IS STARING AT THE CAMERA, NOW AINT THAT JUST FUCKIN SPOOPY

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of my favorite SCPs in a while and on the shortlist of "things here that gave me the genuine willies"

Also, seconding that this makes me want to dig out my copy of Borges.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What about fiction that initially exists in the context of other fiction but someone subsequently writes/creates? To observe the transition would be interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It might be interesting if, in every exploration of nested fictions, you can't go more than 6 levels deep without hitting the static. If you're in a fiction that only exists in a fiction that only exists in a fiction that only exists in a fiction that only exists in The Sopranos, and you try to go deeper, you'll hit the static. If you go into a copy of Goodfellas that exists in the Sopranos, then you can go an extra level deep because Goodfellas exists in our world, but you're still descending the ladder.

Level 1: The Sopranos.
Level 2: a fiction that only exists in the context of The Sopranos. Looks normal.
Level 3: a fiction that only exists in the context of Level 2. Looks normal, but all humans are gone.
Level 4: a fiction that only exists in the context of Level 3. All the characters notice the POV camera, and stare at it.
Level 5: a fiction that only exists in the context of Level 4. Looks like level 3, but also the sky is black and starless, with a red glow.
Level 6: static which eventually resolves into that corridor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a sucker for media SCPs, and I like how you subverted this and made it darker than expected.

Though I wonder whether it would be served well with a collab log or not. I'd love to see what people do with this idea

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Researchers were direcIt was revealed

This makes no sense to me, i think you forgot to clean it up

Also, Dorothy and the Wizard of Oz plot were all mentioned under the Snow White test

Article could benefit by explaining travelling between canons. I might have missed it, but i see no sentence explaining the possibility of switching from the The Sopranos world.

item.title: Snow White Universe
item.content:encoded: The Snow White Universe has a sentence that appears to be incomplete and doesn't elaborate on what the researchers did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Got translated over at the Chinese site!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I got some awesome imagery while reading this. Makes the reader wonder of all the possibilities and leaves some mystery for the mind to fill in.

Great job, man.

I really like this. It creeps me out. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Clever, really digging the idea.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What is with the future tense in the entry after the static? Why isn't it "researchers encountered" instead of "researchers will encounter"? Did they encounter it or not? How do they know what they are going to see before they see it?

Also, I agree with Deci, the new final entry isn't very good.

Also, also I just want to reiterate that the picture of the DVD art isn't working. There's no reason for it to be there (and it shouldn't be labeled as a picture of a disc anyway).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ayy don't mess with a good thing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just noticed that the logs now extend past the Static entry and include "spoopy jumpscare pic" and an entry that caused people to experience "exacerbate[d] latent mental illness".

Downvoted. Back to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This just caused one of my nightmares (er, one of my nightmares was about this. I'm not exactly the most lucid right now). I dunno if you meant this to be full-out horror but congrats, it certainly is terrifying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've rerated to +1 - nice extrapolation of the idea through the new logs.

These - what might be referred "meta-anomalies"

Should that be "what might be referred to as "meta-anomalies""?

Diegetic Date: [REDACTED]

There are so few of these with actual information in them that I think you'd be better off removing them entirely - they don't seem to add much for either the in-universe or out-of-universe reader.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article was okay up until logs. The logs were really fun to read, the way that you used what seemed to be a minor effect into fantastic exploration logs was amazing. So good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i made it a point to avoid this from becoming a list of lost episode creepypastas but if u aint feeling it you aint feeling it. glad it survived the gauntlet since i really love the idea

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I guess the difference is, with 1733, the point is "these people are trapped in a hell from which they'll never escape", while this one is basically "infinite creepypastas". I mean, that hallway image gives me the willies, no lie, but there's just not much point to this in comparison.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: eh I've always had a fascination with the boundaries or unseen places of fictional worlds. i guess it's about exploring strange and fascinating shit that all ties back into a familiar frame of reference, like the idea of a whole world of folksy animated fairy tales you see in the snow white one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same here. I also find it somewhat odd that the image doesn't have a hole, given it isn't described as the DVD artwork. That aside, I really like the concept(s) and the execution is very entertaining. Many pluses to you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Though the final logs are nice and creepy, I feel like this is a less-interesting retread of what 1733 accomplished. Also, I wish you'd written "Bookshelf" on the image.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think one of my only complaints (aside from all the redactions of the Diegetic Dates; why are those redacted?) is that I'd probably prefer a picture of the Sopranos disc labeled "BOOKSHELF", rather than just a generic picture of the Sopranos DVD. It's a little distracting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ayy, I like what you did with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, because I got really confused at first and thought that I had misunderstood what the SCP actually was. I thought, "Wait, is the anomaly not a Sopranos DVD? Is it a thing that lets you go into digital/video worlds, like the remote or something? Did the DVD turn the remote into an anomaly?" It took me a minute to figure it out. Working on the phrasing leading up to it with an eye toward clarity can fix that right up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Clearly connected to your SCP-1733 in some way. Obviously the red light means something. Red shift?

I wonder what would happen if you moved the viewpoint to wherever 1733 is and entered it during testing…

Any reason why the picture is of a ordinary disc? I am not seeing anything that prevents photography of this object.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now it's reminiscent of "Tlön, Uqbar, Orbis Tertius" and that's terrific.

I still think some of the language needs to be tightened to make it clear that the POV can enter screens shown on screen and continue from there to enter other screens and so on, but this is now really getting into the meat of the possibilities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ayyy, i love all the tvs tuned to static.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, you really ran with the criticism! I'm impressed on the turnaround time, to say the least.

Let's see… On the positive side, I feel as if the logs tell a much more cohesive story, and that story has much more of a "creepy" vibe to it than previous. Huge improvement all around, honestly. I like the idea of the Commercial Apocalypse a lot, too - I didn't expect that and it's the first taste of "creepy" in the article.

As for things that don't work as well as they could or areas for possible improvement:

  • The log progression might work better if "Alternate Property Logs" went before "Alternate Media Log". Then it's Sopranos Universe -> Sopranos & Other Stuff -> Other Stuff before we get into the recursive testing.
  • A bunch of people get twitchy when [DATA EXPUNGED] gets tacked on the end of stuff. It's generally agreed to work best as a "Monster in the shadows", where you can kinda make out what it is but it's creepier because you can't quite see the shape of it. It's actually more work to use it correctly than not, simply because you have to set up an expectation or creeping suspicion in the reader's mind and then use the redaction to loosely cover it up with a tablecloth, letting them decide whether they want to uncover it or not (by thinking about it too long or looking for the hints you scattered around). If you just use it as "Oh there's something scary behind that black box, be scared!", it's ineffective and probably counterproductive. You've got about three stuck on the ends of logs that don't work very well; either reduce it to just the last one or try to give the reader more information to creep them out.
  • The recursive inception-style tests were a neat idea, but it needs a little more spit and polish. Through the third recursion, it all works and there's a nice progression of weird -> weirder -> uh oh, but then the next-to-last is a little disappointing (I'd strike it entirely and try moving the "static" test into here, saying that all media was now tuned to static in the Snakebite world, maybe all reflective surfaces as well?). I'm not sure about the final one, but don't just end it on an expungement, or justify it if you do. Perhaps have the camera come out of the hallway into the restaurant at the finale of the Sopranos right before everything usually goes black, but instead the researchers see [DATA EXPUNGED FOR COGNITOHAZARD]. Cleanup in Aisle 3, the new researchers are barred from doing this, and the old researchers are implied to not be around anymore.
  • There's an odd spelling error or two and some slightly awkward phrasing here and there. Those can be cleaned up when you have "fresh eyes" in a day or so and can decide what gets the message across and what could be clearer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: New logs are up lmk what you think

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My thoughts exactly. If the vignette-style notes were reordered and partially rewritten and/or expanded into a proper research log (first testing the scope of the world, then moving into "parallel" fictional worlds, then pushing the boundaries and screwing up as per usual Foundation protocol), we'd have a great execution of a killer idea.

People clearly like this concept, and it's got a ton of potential. The trick would be exploring this potential further.

Some ideas:

  • Having a still-running show that could have been accessed in-world (Simpsons? I forget the time-frame the Sopranos covered) change its canon slightly, and see if the world shifts accordingly, or is "locked" to the existing canon from when the DVD was made,
  • Exploring more unusual media, such as commercials/infomercials, art gallery video exhibits, etc.
  • Can the camera enter computer screens as well? That could get weird fast…
  • Does it work on paintings? Digital art?
  • How about if it flies into a screen of static, bearing in mind that "static" is partially terrestrial RF interference, and partially cosmic background radiation? Would anything unexpected happen?
  • Kids' show universes can be explored here - how might that be subverted?
  • Mirrors?

And I'm sure there's lots of other ways this can be played with. The trick is going to be finding a way that this can be twisted to make the reader uncomfortable, I suppose, since that's the "meat" that's missing from the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept you've got here, but the language isn't quite right yet. And echoing TyGently, the end note is pretty meh. You tipped your hand, but seemingly only as an afterthought, and a bit too obvious.

There are some good chunks, and I'd love to see this SCP punched up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really want to like this. But there are several breaks from clinical tone (hangout being one of them) that just wrench me out of the immersion. I'd say a thorough tightening up of the language would push me over the edge into an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting, but the endings do nothing for me. The note at the end seems useless, and the final part of the first collapsible seems anticlimactic. There seems to be so much you could do with this idea, but in the end not all that much is done with it.

EDIT: After seeing your edits, I think I can safely upvote. Glad to see you really ran with the feedback. +1
